# Results {#sec-results}

## Dataset summary

@tbl-dataset-summary summarizes the two assembled panels.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-dataset-summary
#| tbl-cap: "Summary of the assembled flexible and rigid LTPP IRI datasets (IRI min/max in tables/dataset_summary.csv)."
#| echo: false
import pandas as pd
df = pd.read_csv("../../tables/dataset_summary.csv")
df = df.rename(columns={"N_records": "N", "N_sections": "Sections", "N_states": "States",
                         "IRI_mean": "IRI mean", "IRI_sd": "IRI sd", "Age_mean_yr": "Age mean (yr)"})
df[["Dataset", "N", "Sections", "States", "IRI mean", "IRI sd", "Age mean (yr)"]]
```

Both panels span a comparable range of in-service roughness (@fig-iri-dist), with the familiar right-skew of
a well-maintained highway network: most sections cluster between 1 and 2 m/km, with a thin tail of rougher
outliers that any predictive model has to work hard to capture.

![Distribution of IRI by pavement family.](../../plots/iri_distribution.png){#fig-iri-dist width=95%}

Age and IRI show the expected — and expectedly noisy — relationship (@fig-iri-age): both families roughen
somewhat with age, but the relationship is weak on its own, which is precisely the motivation for a
multivariate model that can weigh age against structure, climate, and accumulated distress simultaneously
rather than reading it off a single scatterplot.

![IRI versus pavement age at the time of survey, by family.](../../plots/iri_vs_age.png){#fig-iri-age width=80%}

## Model comparison

For every pavement family we fit three variants: the primary **structural** model (distress, structure,
climate, and imputed traffic/LTE — no lagged IRI), a **traffic+LTE-ablated** structural model (the same rows
and split, minus those two imputed variables, isolating their marginal contribution), and an **operational**
model that adds the section's own previous IRI reading. @tbl-master reports held-out LightGBM performance
across all of them; @tbl-flex-metrics and @tbl-rigid-metrics give the full four-model comparison (linear,
random forest, Optuna-tuned ANN, Optuna-tuned LightGBM) for the primary structural variant.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-master
#| tbl-cap: "LightGBM held-out R² across every pavement family and model variant (full N/RMSE detail in tables/master_lightgbm_comparison.csv)."
#| echo: false
import pandas as pd
df = pd.read_csv("../../tables/master_lightgbm_comparison.csv")
df["Pavement family"] = df["Pavement family"].str.replace("Rigid, pooled (JPCP+JRCP+CRCP)", "Rigid, pooled", regex=False)
df["Pavement family"] = df["Pavement family"].str.replace("Rigid, JPCP/JRCP only", "Rigid, JPCP only", regex=False)
df["Variant"] = df["Variant"].str.replace("Structural, traffic+LTE ablated", "No traffic/LTE", regex=False)
df["Variant"] = df["Variant"].str.replace("Operational (+ lagged IRI)", "Operational (lag)", regex=False)
df[["Pavement family", "Variant", "R2 (held-out)", "CV R2 mean", "CV R2 std"]]
```

```{python}
#| label: tbl-flex-metrics
#| tbl-cap: "Structural model comparison — flexible (asphalt) pavements (MAE in tables/flexible_structural_model_metrics.csv)."
#| echo: false
import pandas as pd
pd.set_option("display.max_colwidth", None)
short_names = {
    "MEPDG-style Linear Regression": "Linear (M-E style)",
    "Random Forest": "Random Forest",
    "ANN (MLP, Optuna-tuned)": "ANN (tuned)",
    "Gradient-Boosted Ensemble (LightGBM, Optuna-tuned)": "LightGBM (tuned)",
}
df = pd.read_csv("../../tables/flexible_structural_model_metrics.csv")
df["Model"] = df["Model"].replace(short_names)
df = df[["Model", "R2", "RMSE", "Train_time_s", "Speedup_vs_RF"]].round(3)
df.columns = ["Model", "R2", "RMSE", "Time (s)", "vs RF"]
df
```

```{python}
#| label: tbl-rigid-metrics
#| tbl-cap: "Structural model comparison — rigid pavements, pooled JPCP+JRCP+CRCP (MAE in tables/rigid_structural_model_metrics.csv)."
#| echo: false
import pandas as pd
pd.set_option("display.max_colwidth", None)
df = pd.read_csv("../../tables/rigid_structural_model_metrics.csv")
df["Model"] = df["Model"].replace(short_names)
df = df[["Model", "R2", "RMSE", "Train_time_s", "Speedup_vs_RF"]].round(3)
df.columns = ["Model", "R2", "RMSE", "Time (s)", "vs RF"]
df
```

Four findings emerge from @tbl-master. First, **Optuna tuning and MICE-style traffic/LTE imputation
meaningfully lift the structural models** relative to an untuned configuration with traffic/LTE excluded:
flexible-pavement $R^2$ rises from 0.333 to 0.414, and pooled rigid from 0.254 to 0.220 — the rigid figure is
a rare case where systematic tuning *did not* help (discussed below). Second, **removing traffic and LTE costs
a small but consistent
amount of accuracy everywhere** (flexible: 0.414 → 0.395; pooled rigid: 0.220 → 0.203), confirming they
carry real, non-redundant signal rather than being safely ignorable, without them being the dominant driver
either. Third, **the operational (lagged-IRI) variant is dramatically more accurate everywhere**
($R^2=0.698$ flexible, $0.391$ pooled rigid), which is expected — IRI is strongly autocorrelated within a
section — and is reported as a distinct, clearly-labeled model rather than folded into the "structural"
headline number, since the two answer different engineering questions (see @sec-methods). Fourth, **pooling
JPCP/JRCP and CRCP into one rigid model outperforms splitting them**: the JPCP/JRCP-only structural submodel
is actually *worse than predicting the mean* ($R^2=-0.294$, $n=118$ sections), and the CRCP-only submodel,
while positive ($R^2=0.226$), carries enormous cross-validation variance ($\pm0.246$) from having only 61
sections to work with. Splitting a modest sample into two smaller, noisier ones costs more in lost training
diversity than it gains in mechanism-specific homogeneity — an empirical answer, not an assumption, to
whether JPCP and CRCP should share a model.

The rigid-pavement ANN is the clearest illustration of a tree ensemble's advantage in this small-section-count
regime: even after a 40-trial Optuna search over architecture, L2 regularization, and learning rate, it
manages only $R^2=0.009$ (pooled) to $R^2=-1.23$ (CRCP-only) on the structural task — worse, in the CRCP case,
than predicting the sample mean — while LightGBM's built-in leaf-wise regularization stays positive across
every rigid variant. Because the ANN is tuned against the same group-aware cross-validation objective as
LightGBM, its weaker performance reflects a genuine bias-variance trade-off in a few-hundred-section regime
rather than inadequate tuning of the baseline.

::: {.callout-note}
### Why the ensemble wins on speed, not just accuracy
LightGBM's histogram-based split-finding and leaf-wise tree growth make it substantially cheaper to train
than either the ANN (repeated gradient-descent epochs) or the random forest (hundreds of fully-grown,
un-pruned trees). Across every family and variant in @tbl-master, tuned LightGBM trains 1.7$\times$ to
9$\times$ faster than the random forest baseline. For an agency that wants to retrain its IRI model every time
a new LTPP release drops — as opposed to training it once and never touching it again — that speed advantage
compounds.
:::

## Robustness and sensitivity checks

Five further checks accompany the headline structural model, addressing gradient-boosting implementation
choice, split methodology, age provenance, imputation method, and candidate feature additions in turn.

**Gradient-boosting implementation.** @tbl-gbm-compare compares LightGBM against XGBoost [@chen2016xgboost]
and CatBoost [@prokhorenkova2018catboost], each independently tuned with a 25-trial Optuna search against the
same group-aware cross-validation objective as the headline model.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-gbm-compare
#| tbl-cap: "Structural-model R² by gradient-boosting implementation."
#| echo: false
import pandas as pd
pd.read_csv("../../tables/gbm_implementation_comparison.csv")
```

LightGBM is the best- or near-best-performing implementation in three of four cases, and is within 0.03-0.06
$R^2$ of the best alternative in the fourth (JPCP/JRCP-only, where CatBoost edges ahead in an already
low-accuracy regime). The choice among modern gradient-boosting implementations does not change any of this
study's conclusions.

**Split methodology, and why its effect varies by family.** @tbl-leakage reports, for each family, the same
Optuna-tuned hyperparameters evaluated under a naive row-level split and under the group-aware split used
throughout this study, isolating the contribution of splitting strategy alone from any confound with
hyperparameter selection.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-leakage
#| tbl-cap: "Naive-split vs. group-aware-split R², same tuned hyperparameters."
#| echo: false
import pandas as pd
pd.read_csv("../../tables/leakage_comparison_all_families.csv")
```

The inflation from a naive split is not constant: it is negligible for flexible pavements (0.417 vs. 0.414)
but substantial for rigid pavements, and largest of all for the JPCP/JRCP-only submodel (0.355 vs. -0.294, an
inflation of 0.649 $R^2$) — precisely the configuration whose structural feature set explains the least
genuine variance. This is consistent with a specific mechanism rather than a generic warning: when a naive
split allows repeated visits of the same section into both training and test data, the model can recover a
section's persistent, unmeasured characteristics from its training-set visits and apply that section-specific
signature to held-out visits of the *same* section. That recovery is most profitable, and therefore produces
the largest apparent accuracy gain, exactly where the measured covariates explain the smallest share of true
variance, since more of the outcome is then attributable to persistent section identity that only a naive
split allows to leak through. A well-tuned model evaluated on a well-explained outcome (flexible pavements)
has comparatively little unexplained section-specific variance left for a naive split to exploit; a model
facing a poorly-explained outcome (JPCP/JRCP structural roughness) has a great deal, and a naive split
rewards memorizing it. This is a more specific and testable claim than the coarser observation that an early,
less-regularized configuration of the flexible-pavement model showed a large naive-vs-grouped gap (0.52 vs.
0.33): leakage-driven inflation should scale with the fraction of outcome variance the structural features
fail to explain, a pattern @tbl-leakage confirms across four independently-tuned models spanning a wide range
of explanatory power.

**Age provenance, imputation method, and candidate feature additions.** @tbl-sensitivity summarizes three
further checks against the headline structural model.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-sensitivity
#| tbl-cap: "Sensitivity checks against the headline structural model (full detail in tables/sensitivity_checks_all_families.csv)."
#| echo: false
import pandas as pd
df = pd.read_csv("../../tables/sensitivity_checks_all_families.csv")
compact = pd.DataFrame({
    "Pavement family": df["Pavement family"].str.replace("Rigid, JPCP/JRCP only", "Rigid, JPCP only", regex=False),
    "Headline R2": df["Headline R2"],
    "Age-verified (N)": [f"{r:.2f} ({n})" for r, n in
                          zip(df["Age-verified-only R2"], df["Age-verified N sections"])],
    "Multi-imputation": [f"{m:.2f}±{s:.2f}" for m, s in
                          zip(df["Multi-imputation R2 mean"], df["Multi-imputation R2 std"])],
    "Ext. features": df["Extended-features R2"],
})
compact
```

Restricting to records with a verified `INV_AGE.CONSTRUCTION_DATE` (excluding the age-proxy fallback) leaves
only 19-61 sections per family — too few for a stable held-out estimate, and the resulting $R^2$ values swing
widely, including strongly negative, in both directions relative to the full-sample headline figure. We read
this as a sample-size artifact of the restriction itself rather than evidence that the age proxy is
unreliable: with a test set of 4-12 sections, a handful of atypical records can dominate $R^2$. A dedicated
larger sample with universal construction-date coverage would be needed to test the age proxy's effect
directly. Multiple imputation, in contrast, gives a clear and reassuring answer: a five-draw stochastic
multiple-imputation ensemble produces held-out $R^2$ within 0.003-0.05 of the single-imputation pipeline used
throughout, for every family, with the largest discrepancy (CRCP-only, $\pm0.038$ SD) occurring in the
family with the fewest sections. The single-pass MICE approach is not a material source of uncertainty
relative to sampling variability at these sample sizes. Finally, adding a binary base-type indicator
(granular vs. treated/stabilized base) and subgrade plasticity index to the structural feature set *reduces*
held-out $R^2$ in every family (e.g., flexible: 0.414 → 0.346), rather than improving it. Plasticity index is
populated for only 17-18% of records nationally, and imputing a variable at that missingness level appears to
introduce more noise than signal once passed through the same training-fold-only imputer used for traffic and
LTE; the binary base-type indicator, while fully observed, does not compensate. We report this as a genuine
negative result: at least for these two candidate variables under this imputation strategy, expanding the
structural feature set beyond the headline set does not improve on it.

## What drives IRI: SHAP feature attribution

@fig-shap-flex and @fig-shap-rigid show SHAP summary plots for the trained structural ensemble models. Each
point is one held-out record; its horizontal position is that record's SHAP value (its feature's contribution,
in m/km, to the predicted IRI for that specific record), and its color is the feature's raw value (red = high,
blue = low).

![SHAP summary — flexible pavements (structural model). High transverse crack length and patching area push predicted IRI up; the model recovers this relationship directly from data.](../../plots/flexible_structural_shap_summary.png){#fig-shap-flex width=90%}

![SHAP summary — rigid pavements, pooled (structural model). Wheel-path faulting and FWD load-transfer efficiency dominate, mirroring the coefficient structure of the NCHRP 1-37A Appendix PP JPCP smoothness equation while adding the mechanistic variable the classical equation only proxies indirectly.](../../plots/rigid_structural_shap_summary.png){#fig-shap-rigid width=90%}

For flexible pavements, MEPDG transverse crack length is the single strongest predictor, followed by patching
area and mean annual temperature — a data-driven confirmation of exactly the distress hierarchy that
@ara2004smoothness's hand-fit equation assumed by design, plus a climate interaction (temperature) that the
fixed-form linear model cannot represent on its own. For rigid pavements, wheel-path faulting dominates by a
wide margin, again mirroring Appendix PP's own JPCP smoothness equation, followed by bound-layer thickness,
transverse joint spalling, mean annual temperature, and precipitation — plausibly a proxy for pumping and
moisture-related loss of support beneath joints, a mechanism the classical equation does not represent
explicitly at all. FWD load-transfer efficiency ranks 10th of 14 features: a real, non-trivial contribution
(consistent with the ablation result earlier in this chapter) rather than a dominant one, with low LTE (poor
joint performance) pushing
predicted IRI up, exactly the mechanistic pathway (loss of aggregate interlock or dowel efficiency → faulting
→ roughness) that Appendix PP's equation encodes only implicitly through the faulting term alone. That LTE
adds real signal without dominating is itself informative: faulting already captures most of what LTE would
explain, since faulting is downstream of load-transfer loss — the two are correlated symptoms of the same
mechanism, so LTE's marginal contribution *on top of* faulting is modest by construction, not because load
transfer is unimportant to rigid-pavement roughness.

The two climate variables warrant a more mechanistic reading than "climate matters." For flexible pavements,
mean annual temperature interacts with cracking rather than substituting for it in the SHAP plot (@fig-shap-flex):
its effect is concentrated among records that already carry non-trivial transverse crack length, consistent
with the well-established sensitivity of asphalt binder stiffness to temperature — a colder climate embrittles
the binder and accelerates thermal (transverse) cracking for a given traffic and structural exposure, so
temperature acts as an effect modifier on the crack-length–IRI relationship rather than an independent driver.
For rigid pavements, precipitation's contribution is concentrated among high-faulting records, which is the
pattern implied by the classical erosion mechanism underlying pumping: water that infiltrates through joints
or shoulders erodes fines from the base or subgrade, and repeated heavy axle loads pump the resulting slurry
out from under the slab, progressively reducing support directly beneath the joint and increasing differential
deflection across it. This is precisely the moisture-related loss-of-support mechanism that the M-E site
factor $SF = \text{Age} \times (1+FI) \times (1+P_{200})/10^6$ [@ara2004smoothness] was constructed to
proxy in aggregate rather than represent as a direct interaction with faulting; the SHAP-based model recovers
the interaction directly from the joint distribution of precipitation and faulting rather than through a
pre-specified multiplicative form.

Notably, the model recovers this structure **without being given the M-E equation's functional form in
advance** — it is discovered from the joint distribution of distress, climate, LTE, and IRI across hundreds of
LTPP sections. This agreement with established mechanistic-empirical relationships is a useful check on the
SHAP explanation itself: a data-driven attribution that recovers known pavement physics is more credible than
one that cannot be reconciled with prior engineering knowledge.
